title: Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average
subtitle: Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.
caption:
Monthly sea ice extent in the Arctic, decadal average

Each point represents the monthly average sea ice extent, averaged across all years within the decade. The current decade is highlighted in red, with the current year shown in black for comparison.

This line chart displays the monthly average sea ice extent in the Arctic, measured in millions of square kilometers, across five decadal averages and the current year. The horizontal axis represents months from January (1) to December (12), while the vertical axis ranges from 4 million km² to 16 million km². The data reveals a consistent seasonal cycle where ice extent peaks in the early months and reaches a minimum in late summer. The 1980s, represented by the lightest blue line, consistently show the highest extent, peaking near 16 million km² in March and dropping to approximately 7.5 million km² in September. Subsequent decades—the 1990s, 2000s, and 2010s—appear as progressively darker blue lines situated lower on the chart, indicating a reduction in ice extent over time. The 2020s, highlighted in red, sit below the previous decades, with a peak around 14.5 million km² and a minimum near 4.5 million km² in September. The black line, representing 2025, tracks closely with the 2020s average, starting near 13 million km² in January and falling to roughly 11.5 million km² by December. While the note indicates that data for 1987 and 1988 each miss one month, the decadal averages presented cover the full twelve-month cycle. The visual gap between the lightest blue line (1980s) and the red line (2020s) is most pronounced during the summer months, particularly from June through October.
